/home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F: 320 - 536
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


320:          DO s = 1, nnp%ang(ind)%n_symfgrp


321:             n_symf_s = nnp%ang(ind)%symfgrp(s)%n_symf


322:             ! Per-thread persistent buffers grow lazily; THREADPRIVATE arrays sized together.


323:             IF (.NOT. ALLOCATED(scratch_sym) .OR. .NOT. ALLOCATED(local_y) .OR. &


324:                 scratch_max_sym < n_symf_s) THEN


325:                IF (ALLOCATED(scratch_sym)) DEALLOCATE(scratch_sym)


326:                IF (ALLOCATED(scratch_forcetmp)) DEALLOCATE(scratch_forcetmp)


327:                IF (ALLOCATED(scratch_force3tmp)) DEALLOCATE(scratch_force3tmp)


328:                IF (ALLOCATED(local_y)) DEALLOCATE(local_y)


329:                IF (ALLOCATED(local_fi)) DEALLOCATE(local_fi)


330:                IF (ALLOCATED(local_stress)) DEALLOCATE(local_stress)


331:                ALLOCATE(scratch_sym(n_symf_s), scratch_forcetmp(3, n_symf_s), &


332:                         scratch_force3tmp(3, 3, n_symf_s))


333:                ALLOCATE(local_y(n_symf_s), local_fi(3, n_symf_s), local_stress(3, 3, n_symf_s))


334:                scratch_max_sym = n_symf_s


335:             END IF


336:             symtmp => scratch_sym(1:n_symf_s)


337:             force3tmp => scratch_force3tmp(:, :, 1:n_symf_s)


338:             ! Reset thread-local accumulators


339:             local_y(1:n_symf_s) = 0.0_dp


340:             local_fi(:, 1:n_symf_s) = 0.0_dp


341:             IF (has_stress) local_stress(:, :, 1:n_symf_s) = 0.0_dp


342: 


343:             ASSOCIATE (sg => nnp%ang(ind)%symfgrp(s))


344:             ! Per-j cutoff value, derivative, and unit vector are k-loop


345:             ! invariants.  scratch_* is shared across threads, so the realloc


346:             ! check runs under OMP SINGLE and the precompute is then split


347:             ! across the team with OMP DO.


348:             !$OMP SINGLE


349:             fc_needed = neighbor%n_ang1(s)


350:             IF (sg%ele(1) /= sg%ele(2)) fc_needed = MAX(fc_needed, neighbor%n_ang2(s))


351:             IF (scratch_max_fc < fc_needed) THEN


352:                IF (ALLOCATED(scratch_fc1)) &


353:                   DEALLOCATE(scratch_fc1, scratch_dfc1, scratch_fc2, scratch_dfc2, &


354:                              scratch_dr1dx, scratch_dr2dx)


355:                ALLOCATE(scratch_fc1(fc_needed), scratch_dfc1(fc_needed))


356:                ALLOCATE(scratch_fc2(fc_needed), scratch_dfc2(fc_needed))


357:                ALLOCATE(scratch_dr1dx(3, fc_needed), scratch_dr2dx(3, fc_needed))


358:                scratch_max_fc = fc_needed


359:             END IF


360:             !$OMP END SINGLE


361:             ! implicit barrier ensures the (re)allocation is visible to every thread


362:             SELECT CASE (cut_type)


363:             CASE (nnp_cut_cos)


364:                !$OMP DO SCHEDULE(static)


365:                DO j = 1, neighbor%n_ang1(s)


366:                   r1 = neighbor%dist_ang1(4, j, s)


367:                   fc_tmp = r1*sg%pi_over_cut


368:                   scratch_fc1(j) = 0.5_dp*(minimax_cos(fc_tmp, y1=dfc_tmp) + 1.0_dp)


369:                   scratch_dfc1(j) = 0.5_dp*dfc_tmp*sg%pi_over_cut


370:                   r1inv = 1.0_dp/r1


371:                   scratch_dr1dx(1, j) = neighbor%dist_ang1(1, j, s)*r1inv


372:                   scratch_dr1dx(2, j) = neighbor%dist_ang1(2, j, s)*r1inv


373:                   scratch_dr1dx(3, j) = neighbor%dist_ang1(3, j, s)*r1inv


374:                END DO


375:                !$OMP END DO


376:                IF (sg%ele(1) /= sg%ele(2)) THEN


377:                   !$OMP DO SCHEDULE(static)


378:                   DO k = 1, neighbor%n_ang2(s)


379:                      r2 = neighbor%dist_ang2(4, k, s)


380:                      fc_tmp = r2*sg%pi_over_cut


381:                      scratch_fc2(k) = 0.5_dp*(minimax_cos(fc_tmp, y1=dfc_tmp) + 1.0_dp)


382:                      scratch_dfc2(k) = 0.5_dp*dfc_tmp*sg%pi_over_cut


383:                      r2inv = 1.0_dp/r2


384:                      scratch_dr2dx(1, k) = neighbor%dist_ang2(1, k, s)*r2inv


385:                      scratch_dr2dx(2, k) = neighbor%dist_ang2(2, k, s)*r2inv


386:                      scratch_dr2dx(3, k) = neighbor%dist_ang2(3, k, s)*r2inv


387:                   END DO


388:                   !$OMP END DO


389:                END IF


390:             CASE (nnp_cut_tanh)


391:                !$OMP DO SCHEDULE(static)


392:                DO j = 1, neighbor%n_ang1(s)


393:                   r1 = neighbor%dist_ang1(4, j, s)


394:                   fc_tmp = 1.0_dp - r1*sg%inv_cut


395:                   tanh_v = minimax_tanh(fc_tmp)


396:                   scratch_fc1(j) = tanh_v**3


397:                   scratch_dfc1(j) = (-3.0_dp*sg%inv_cut)*(tanh_v**2 - tanh_v**4)


398:                   r1inv = 1.0_dp/r1


399:                   scratch_dr1dx(1, j) = neighbor%dist_ang1(1, j, s)*r1inv


400:                   scratch_dr1dx(2, j) = neighbor%dist_ang1(2, j, s)*r1inv


401:                   scratch_dr1dx(3, j) = neighbor%dist_ang1(3, j, s)*r1inv


402:                END DO


403:                !$OMP END DO


404:                IF (sg%ele(1) /= sg%ele(2)) THEN


405:                   !$OMP DO SCHEDULE(static)


406:                   DO k = 1, neighbor%n_ang2(s)


407:                      r2 = neighbor%dist_ang2(4, k, s)


408:                      fc_tmp = 1.0_dp - r2*sg%inv_cut


409:                      tanh_v = minimax_tanh(fc_tmp)


410:                      scratch_fc2(k) = tanh_v**3


411:                      scratch_dfc2(k) = (-3.0_dp*sg%inv_cut)*(tanh_v**2 - tanh_v**4)


412:                      r2inv = 1.0_dp/r2


413:                      scratch_dr2dx(1, k) = neighbor%dist_ang2(1, k, s)*r2inv


414:                      scratch_dr2dx(2, k) = neighbor%dist_ang2(2, k, s)*r2inv


415:                      scratch_dr2dx(3, k) = neighbor%dist_ang2(3, k, s)*r2inv


416:                   END DO


417:                   !$OMP END DO


418:                END IF


419:             END SELECT


420:             ! implicit barrier at end of last !$OMP DO publishes scratch_fc*/dr* to all threads


421: 


422:             IF (sg%ele(1) == sg%ele(2)) THEN


423:                ! Triangular k-loop (k = j+1..n_ang1) is not COLLAPSE-able.


424:                ! Earlier j carries more work; dynamic scheduling with a small


425:                ! chunk handles the imbalance with low overhead.


426:                !$OMP DO SCHEDULE(dynamic, 4)


427:                DO j = 1, neighbor%n_ang1(s)


428:                   rvect1 = neighbor%dist_ang1(1:3, j, s)


429:                   r1 = neighbor%dist_ang1(4, j, s)


430:                   DO k = j + 1, neighbor%n_ang1(s)


431:                      rvect2 = neighbor%dist_ang1(1:3, k, s)


432:                      r2 = neighbor%dist_ang1(4, k, s)


433:                      rvect3 = rvect2 - rvect1


434:                      r3sq = rvect3(1)*rvect3(1) + rvect3(2)*rvect3(2) + rvect3(3)*rvect3(3)


435:                      IF (r3sq < sg%cutoff_sq) THEN


436:                         r3 = SQRT(r3sq)


437:                         jj = neighbor%ind_ang1(j, s)


438:                         kk = neighbor%ind_ang1(k, s)


439:                         CALL nnp_calc_ang(nnp, ind, s, rvect1, rvect2, rvect3, &


440:                                           r1, r2, r3, symtmp, force3tmp, &


441:                                           fc1_in=scratch_fc1(j), fc2_in=scratch_fc1(k), &


442:                                           dfc1dr_in=scratch_dfc1(j), dfc2dr_in=scratch_dfc1(k), &


443:                                           dr1dx_in=scratch_dr1dx(:, j), dr2dx_in=scratch_dr1dx(:, k))


444:                         DO sf = 1, n_symf_s


445:                            m = off + sg%symf(sf)


446:                            DO l = 1, 3


447:                               local_fi(l, sf) = local_fi(l, sf) + force3tmp(l, 1, sf)


448:                               !$OMP ATOMIC UPDATE


449:                               dsymdxyz(m, l, jj) = dsymdxyz(m, l, jj) + force3tmp(l, 2, sf)

452:                            END DO


453:                            IF (has_stress) THEN


454:                               DO l = 1, 3


455:                                  local_stress(1, l, sf) = local_stress(1, l, sf) &


456:                                     - rvect1(1)*force3tmp(l, 2, sf) - rvect2(1)*force3tmp(l, 3, sf)


[...]

466:                   END DO


[...]


474:                !$OMP DO COLLAPSE(2) SCHEDULE(dynamic, 16)


475:                DO j = 1, neighbor%n_ang1(s)


476:                   DO k = 1, neighbor%n_ang2(s)


477:                      rvect1 = neighbor%dist_ang1(1:3, j, s)


478:                      r1 = neighbor%dist_ang1(4, j, s)


479:                      rvect2 = neighbor%dist_ang2(1:3, k, s)


480:                      r2 = neighbor%dist_ang2(4, k, s)


481:                      rvect3 = rvect2 - rvect1


482:                      r3sq = rvect3(1)*rvect3(1) + rvect3(2)*rvect3(2) + rvect3(3)*rvect3(3)


483:                      IF (r3sq < sg%cutoff_sq) THEN


484:                         r3 = SQRT(r3sq)


485:                         jj = neighbor%ind_ang1(j, s)


486:                         kk = neighbor%ind_ang2(k, s)


487:                         CALL nnp_calc_ang(nnp, ind, s, rvect1, rvect2, rvect3, &


488:                                           r1, r2, r3, symtmp, force3tmp, &


489:                                           fc1_in=scratch_fc1(j), fc2_in=scratch_fc2(k), &

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 730.00 to 34.00 cycles (21.47x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop


Vectorization


Your loop is probably not vectorized.
Only 11% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).


Details


Store and arithmetical SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).


Workaround


Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


512-bits vectorization


On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.


Workaround


Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high


  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 730.00 cycles. At this rate:

  • 3% of peak load performance is reached (4.32 out of 128.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 5% of peak store performance is reached (3.73 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 730.00 to 569.00 cycles (1.28x speedup).


Workaround


Try to reduce the loop size:

  • Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:

    • use axHost or xHost
  • Reduce the loop unroll factor:

    • example: replace !DIR$ UNROLL(4) with !DIR$ UNROLL(2) or insert !DIR$ NOUNROLL before loop


ASM code


In the binary file, the address of the loop is: 1bf4249

Functionnnp_calc_acsf

          
Source file and linesnnp_acsf.F:320-536

        
Modulecp2k.psmp

        

The loop is defined in /home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F:320-456,463-466,474-504,511-512,519-536.

Analyzed code is defined in /home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F:320-343,348-367,375-380,388,391-394,403-408,417,422,426-427,445,449,474-476,519-522,526,531-536.

Warnings:
  • Non-innermost loop: analyzing only self part (ignoring child loops).
  • Ignoring paths for analysis
  • Failed to get the number of paths (too many, greater than internal limit = 100000)

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 730.00 to 34.00 cycles (21.47x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this loop


Vectorization


Your loop is probably not vectorized.
Only 11% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).


Details


Store and arithmetical SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).


Workaround


Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


512-bits vectorization


On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.


Workaround


Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high


  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

Workaround


Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

Type of elements and instruction set


No instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on FP elements. This loop is probably writing/copying data or processing integer elements.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 730.00 cycles. At this rate:

  • 3% of peak load performance is reached (4.32 out of 128.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 5% of peak store performance is reached (3.73 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 730.00 to 569.00 cycles (1.28x speedup).


Workaround


Try to reduce the loop size:

  • Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:

    • use axHost or xHost
  • Reduce the loop unroll factor:

    • example: replace !DIR$ UNROLL(4) with !DIR$ UNROLL(2) or insert !DIR$ NOUNROLL before loop
